mnemonics.title: PEMDAS for Operators
mnemonics.content: Python Order: ** (Exponent) → - (Negation) → * / // % (multiplication/division) → + - (addition/subtraction). Remember: Please Excuse My Dear Aunt Sally includes parentheses too!

mnemonics.title: Variable Naming Rules
mnemonics.content: Variable names: Start with letter (a-z, A-Z) or underscore (_), followed by letters, digits, or underscores. CANNOT start with digit. Mnemonic: 'Letters Are Friends, Underscores Join, Numbers Don't Lead'.

mnemonics.title: Truthy Values
mnemonics.content: Falsy values: None, False, 0, 0.0, '', [], {}, set(). Everything else is truthy! Mnemonic: 'None Few Zeroes Empty Containers' (None, False, 0, empty collections).

acronyms.usage: Documents providing guidelines and information about Python. PEP 8 is the style guide for Python code.
The runtime environment that executes Python bytecode, converting source code to machine-executable instructions.
Interactive programming environment in Python shell where you type code and see immediate results.
Python's basic IDE that comes bundled with Python for writing and running code.

visualTips.visualization: Think of indentation like building floors - each indent level is a new floor. The wall (code) on floor 1 belongs to floor 1. You can't have a window on floor 3 without being on floor 3 first!
Think of variables as sticky labels attached to objects in memory, not boxes containing values. When you do y = x, you're putting a second label on the same object, not copying it.
Think of operators as a ladder - highest precedence at top (**), working down (+,-). Calculations start from top of ladder and go down, left to right at same level.

analogies.analogy: Like a dynamic phone number that can change type - one moment it's a number for calling, next it's stored as text message. Python figures out what type data is when it runs, not before.
Giving someone your house key (reference) vs building them a new house (copy). When y = x, y gets a copy of your key - you both enter the same house. Changes by either affect the same house.
Think of Python like a simultaneous translator - speaks directly to the computer line by line (interpreted), while languages like C/Java need a full manuscript translated first (compiled).
